case_name: Minors v. Toronto Sun Publishing Corporation
text:
Minors v. Toronto Sun Publishing Corporation
Collection
Decisions of the Court of Appeal
Date
1999-06-30
Docket numbers
C26963
Judges
Osborne, Coulter Arthur Anthony; Catzman, Marvin Adrian; Charron, Louise Vivianne
Subject
Civil
Decision Content
DATE: 19990630 DOCKET: C26963 COURT OF APPEAL FOR ONTARIO OSBORNE A.C.J.O., CATZMAN and CHARRON JJ.A. BETWEEN: ) ) Charles Campbell, ARNOLD MINORS ) Winston G. Mattis and ) Richard Elliott, Plaintiff ) for the appellant (Appellant) ) ) - and - ) ) Joe Conforti and THE TORONTO SUN PUBLISHING ) Carolyn Silver, CORPORATION, HARTLEY STEWARD, ) for the respondents JOHN DOWNING, CHRISTIE ) BLATCHFORD, JAMES WALLACE, ) JEFF HARDER, ROBERT BENZIE, ) TRACY NESDOLY, ANDY DONATO ) and ERIC ) ) Heard: June 28, 1999 Defendants ) (Respondents) ) ) ) On appeal from the judgment of the Honourable Mr. Justice N. D. McRae dated February 26, 1997 BY THE COURT: [1] The appellant Arnold Minors sued The Toronto Sun Publishing Corporation (“The Sun”) and a number of individual reporters for defamation in relation to twenty-eight articles, cartoons, or editorials published by The Sun in October and November, 1994. At all relevant times, Minors was an organizational and training consultant on equity issues. He worked mainly for governments and non-profit organizations. He also served as a commissioner on the Metropolitan Toronto Board of Police Commissioners. [2] The publications which the appellant alleges were defamatory relate to three topics: (1) the assertion that Minors told a group of Crown Attorneys during a training seminar which concerned racism that the Holocaust was not a racist act, (2) the assertion that Minors walked past a police officer in distress without offering any assistance during the “Albert Moses incident” and (3) the assertion that Minors called the Metropolitan Toronto police an occupying army. [3] The trial was held before McRae J. without a jury. After twenty-one days of hearing, the trial judge dismissed the action. He found that the publications correctly stated the facts and constituted fair comment on those facts. He also held that Minors had failed to prove malice on the part of any of the defendants. The appellant appeals on several grounds. [4] The appellant’s first and main contention is that the trial judge erred in failing to find the respondent liable for the publication of articles that created the false innuendo that he is anti-Semitic. [5] The appellant’s position at trial was that he had never stated that the Holocaust was not a racist act during the course of seminars delivered to a number of Crown attorneys. The trial judge noted in his reasons that this was the most contentious issue at trial and that more than half the evidence at trial was led with respect to this crucial question. The trial judge held that this question was easily resolved because the defence had “proven beyond question that Minors did say that the Holocaust was not racist.” [6] The appellant, in his factum, argues that the trial judge erred in finding that Minors had in fact stated that the Holocaust was not a racist act as published by the respondent in several of the impugned articles. However, this ground of appeal was not pursued in oral argument and the appeal was argued on the basis that this finding of fact would not be overturned by this court. [7] In our view, counsel for the appellant was correct in adopting this position. The trial judge's finding on this issue was amply supported by the evidence. It follows from this finding of fact that no liability can arise from the publication of the appellant’s statement about the Holocaust. [8] The appellant argues nonetheless that the reporting by The Sun conveyed the innuendo that Minors is anti-Semitic and that, consequently, it was incumbent upon The Sun at trial not only to prove that Minors had in fact made the Holocaust remark but also to prove the truth of this innuendo. It is submitted that the innuendo arose from the repeated publication of the fact that Minors had said that the Holocaust was not a racist act and from two references to the appellant’s “anti-Semitic teachings” in the impugned publications. [9] This argument was raised at trial as an alternative basis for liability with respect to the publications concerning the Holocaust remark. The trial judge was of the view that the argument was “without merit.” He stated that “the words quoted have a meaning which is plain and obvious in themselves” and that “Minors knew as early as March 1994 that his limited definition of racism was hurtful, not only to the Jewish community but also to others.” [10] Counsel for the appellant submits that the trial judge erred in law in not giving effect to this alternative argument. [11] In our view, the trial judge made no error in principle and was correct in his conclusion on this issue. This is not a case where words have been quoted out of their reasonable context thereby creating a false and defamatory innuendo. The appellant's statements about the Holocaust were accurately reproduced in their proper context. The Sun cannot be held responsible for any inference a reader might draw from an accurately reported fact. We agree with the trial judge that, on the facts of this case, this argument is without merit. [12] We are also of the view that the trial judge’s conclusions with respect to the other impugned publications are supported by the evidence. All of the publications are defensible either as statements that are substantially true or as fair comment on those true facts. Given those conclusions, it was not incumbent upon the trial judge to sort out which publications consisted of fact and which consisted of comment. [13] Finally, we see no error in the trial judge’s findings on the issue of malice. He properly exercised his discretion in admitting some of The Sun's earlier publications on this issue and in excluding others for lack of relevance. His finding that there was no malice is entirely supported by the evidence. [14] Consequently, we are of the view that the appeal cannot succeed. The appellant has not satisfied us that the trial judge made any material error in principle. In our view, his conclusions are based on findings of fact that are amply supported by the evidence. There is no basis for appellate intervention. The appeal is dismissed with costs.
RELEASED: June 30, 1999
